title_en: Women Companions: Their Scholarship and Sacrifice
title_id: Para Sahabiyah: Keilmuan dan Pengorbanan Mereka
content_en:
Beyond the Prophet's wives, the women Companions of the early Muslim community represent one of the most remarkable assemblages of courageous, learned, and spiritually committed women in human history. Ghadanfar's survey of these women challenges the assumption that Islamic history is primarily a male narrative, revealing a community in which women participated fully in the religious, educational, and military life of the early Ummah.

The scholarly contributions of women Companions extended far beyond Aisha. Umm Salamah narrated approximately 300 hadiths; Umm Atiyya al-Ansariyyah specialized in the regulations of funeral washing and women's prayer obligations; Asma bint Abi Bakr was a primary narrator of early seerah events and survived to advanced old age with her knowledge intact. These women taught men and women alike, and their narrations were recorded with the same isnad precision as those of male narrators.

Sumayyah bint Khayyat, the mother of Ammar ibn Yasir, holds the honor of being the first martyr in Islamic history — killed by Abu Jahl for refusing to abandon her faith. She was an elderly enslaved woman with no tribal protection, who withstood torture that younger, stronger individuals would have found unbearable, and who chose death over apostasy. Her martyrdom established the principle that the weakest members of the community — the old, the enslaved, the female — were capable of the highest acts of faith, and that their sacrifice was honored by the same divine recognition given to the greatest warriors.

Nusaybah bint Ka'b (Umm Umarah) is perhaps the most celebrated female warrior of early Islam. At the Battle of Uhud, when the Muslim lines broke and many fled, she remained defending the Prophet personally with bow, spear, and sword. She received multiple wounds and continued fighting through them. The Prophet reportedly said he never looked left or right during the battle without seeing her defending him. Her subsequent participation in multiple campaigns, including the Riddah wars where she lost her hand, established a model of female military commitment that Islamic tradition has always honored.

Khansa — the great poet Tamadur bint Amr — was famous in pre-Islamic Arabia for her elegies (ritha') mourning the death of her brother Sakhr. When she converted to Islam, she channeled that poetic gift into Islamic expression. At the Battle of Qadisiyyah, she sent all four of her sons into battle with the admonition to fight bravely for Islam — and all four were martyred. Her response — expressing gratitude to Allah for honoring her with their martyrdom — represents a spiritual fortitude that exceeded even the most celebrated male stoicism of the ancient world.

Ghadanfar's conclusion reflects on the significance of these women for contemporary Muslim communities. Their scholarship challenges any tradition that marginalizes women's religious learning. Their sacrifice challenges any tradition that exempts women from the responsibilities of faith. Their diversity of contribution — scholars, warriors, poets, nurses, political advisors — challenges any reduction of Islamic women's roles to a single domestic function. They are, collectively, the fullest possible answer to the question of what Islamic women are capable of.
content_id:
Di luar istri-istri Nabi ﷺ, para Sahabiyah dari komunitas Muslim awal mewakili salah satu kumpulan perempuan-perempuan paling luar biasa yang berani, terpelajar, dan berkomitmen secara spiritual dalam sejarah manusia. Survei Ghadanfar tentang perempuan-perempuan ini menantang asumsi bahwa sejarah Islam pada dasarnya adalah narasi laki-laki, mengungkapkan komunitas di mana perempuan berpartisipasi sepenuhnya dalam kehidupan keagamaan, pendidikan, dan militer Umat awal.

Kontribusi-kontribusi keilmuan para Sahabiyah jauh melampaui Aisyah. Umm Salamah meriwayatkan sekitar 300 hadis; Umm Atiyyah al-Ansariyyah mengkhususkan diri dalam peraturan-peraturan memandikan jenazah dan kewajiban-kewajiban shalat perempuan; Asma binti Abu Bakr adalah perawi utama peristiwa-peristiwa sirah awal dan bertahan hingga usia lanjut dengan pengetahuannya tetap utuh. Perempuan-perempuan ini mengajar laki-laki maupun perempuan, dan narasi-narasi mereka dicatat dengan presisi isnad yang sama seperti para perawi laki-laki.

Sumayyah binti Khayyat, ibu dari Ammar bin Yasir, memegang kehormatan sebagai syahid pertama dalam sejarah Islam — dibunuh oleh Abu Jahal karena menolak untuk meninggalkan imannya. Ia adalah seorang perempuan tua yang diperbudak tanpa perlindungan suku, yang menanggung siksaan yang oleh individu-individu yang lebih muda dan lebih kuat pun tidak dapat ditanggung, dan yang memilih kematian daripada kemurtadan. Kesyahidannya menetapkan prinsip bahwa anggota-anggota komunitas yang paling lemah — yang tua, yang diperbudak, yang perempuan — mampu melakukan tindakan-tindakan iman tertinggi, dan bahwa pengorbanan mereka dihormati oleh pengakuan ilahi yang sama yang diberikan kepada para pejuang terbesar.

Nusaibah binti Ka'b (Umm Umarah) mungkin adalah pejuang perempuan yang paling dirayakan dari Islam awal. Pada Perang Uhud, ketika barisan-barisan Muslim pecah dan banyak yang melarikan diri, ia tetap tinggal mempertahankan Nabi ﷺ secara pribadi dengan busur, tombak, dan pedang. Ia menerima banyak luka dan terus bertarung melaluinya. Nabi ﷺ dilaporkan berkata bahwa beliau tidak pernah melihat ke kiri atau ke kanan selama pertempuran tanpa melihatnya membela beliau. Partisipasinya berikutnya dalam beberapa kampanye, termasuk Perang Riddah di mana ia kehilangan tangannya, menetapkan model komitmen militer perempuan yang selalu dihormati oleh tradisi Islam.

Khansa — penyair besar Tamadur binti Amr — terkenal di Arabia pra-Islam karena elegi-eleginya (ritsaa') yang meratapi kematian saudaranya Shakhr. Ketika ia masuk Islam, ia menyalurkan bakat puitis itu ke dalam ekspresi Islam. Pada Perang Qadisiyyah, ia mengirim semua empat putranya ke medan perang dengan nasihat untuk berjuang dengan berani demi Islam — dan keempat-empatnya gugur sebagai syahid. Responsnya — mengungkapkan rasa syukur kepada Allah karena telah menghormatinya dengan kesyahidan mereka — mewakili keteguhan spiritual yang melampaui bahkan stoisisme laki-laki yang paling dirayakan dari dunia kuno.

Kesimpulan Ghadanfar merefleksikan signifikansi perempuan-perempuan ini bagi komunitas-komunitas Muslim kontemporer. Keilmuan mereka menantang tradisi mana pun yang memarjinalkan pembelajaran agama perempuan. Pengorbanan mereka menantang tradisi mana pun yang mengecualikan perempuan dari tanggung jawab-tanggung jawab iman. Keragaman kontribusi mereka — ulama, pejuang, penyair, perawat, penasihat politik — menantang penyederhanaan peran-peran perempuan Islam ke dalam satu fungsi domestik tunggal. Mereka adalah, secara kolektif, jawaban selengkap mungkin atas pertanyaan tentang apa yang mampu dilakukan oleh perempuan-perempuan Islam.
content_ar:
Beyond النبي ﷺ's wives, the women الصحابة of the early Muslim community represent one of the most remarkable assemblages of courageous, learned, and spiritually committed women in human history. Ghadanfar's survey of these women challenges the assumption that Islamic history is primarily a male narrative, revealing a community in which women participated fully in the religious, educational, and military life of the early Ummah.

The scholarly contributions of women الصحابة extended far beyond Aisha. Umm Salamah narrated approximately 300 hadiths; Umm Atiyya al-Ansariyyah specialized in the regulations of funeral washing and women's prayer obligations; Asma bint Abi Bakr was a primary narrator of early seerah events and survived to advanced old age with her knowledge intact. These women taught men and women alike, and their narrations were recorded with the same isnad precision as those of male narrators.

Sumayyah bint Khayyat, the mother of Ammar ibn Yasir, holds the honor of being the first martyr in Islamic history — killed by Abu Jahl for refusing to abandon her faith. She was an elderly enslaved woman with no tribal protection, who withstood torture that younger, stronger individuals would have found unbearable, and who chose death over apostasy. Her martyrdom established the principle that the weakest members of the community — the old, the enslaved, the female — were capable of the highest acts of faith, and that their sacrifice was honored by the same divine recognition given to the greatest warriors.

Nusaybah bint Ka'b (Umm Umarah) is perhaps the most celebrated female warrior of early Islam. At the Battle of Uhud, when the Muslim lines broke and many fled, she remained defending النبي ﷺ personally with bow, spear, and sword. She received multiple wounds and continued fighting through them. النبي ﷺ reportedly said he never looked left or right during the battle without seeing her defending him. Her subsequent participation in multiple campaigns, including the Riddah wars where she lost her hand, established a model of female military commitment that Islamic tradition has always honored.

Khansa — the great poet Tamadur bint Amr — was famous in pre-Islamic Arabia for her elegies (ritha') mourning the death of her brother Sakhr. When she converted to Islam, she channeled that poetic gift into Islamic expression. At the Battle of Qadisiyyah, she sent all four of her sons into battle with the admonition to fight bravely for Islam — and all four were martyred. Her response — expressing gratitude to الله for honoring her with their martyrdom — represents a spiritual fortitude that exceeded even the most celebrated male stoicism of the ancient world.

Ghadanfar's conclusion reflects on the significance of these women for contemporary Muslim communities. Their scholarship challenges any tradition that marginalizes women's religious learning. Their sacrifice challenges any tradition that exempts women from the responsibilities of faith. Their diversity of contribution — scholars, warriors, poets, nurses, political advisors — challenges any reduction of Islamic women's roles to a single domestic function. They are, collectively, the fullest possible answer to the question of what Islamic women are capable of.
